concept_name: Acceleration as a Function of Time — a = a(t)
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 7.1 — Non-uniform Acceleration; split from non_uniform_acceleration bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: A driver on a quiet stretch of NH-48 who decides to play with the accelerator. He presses it gradually, releases gently, presses again. The car's acceleration at any instant is not a single number — it depends on which moment you sample. Five seconds in, the accelerator is lightly pressed, so a is small. Ten seconds in, the driver has floored it, so a is large. The acceleration is a function of time: a(t), NOT a constant. The SUV-5-5-5 kinematic equations no longer work. Calculus takes over.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A Mumbai Metro train starting from Andheri station. The motorman does not engage full power at once — the current ramps up linearly over the first ten seconds. So the train's acceleration rises from zero to its peak value smoothly. Passengers feel the push growing, not jolting. To predict velocity at every moment, you must integrate the ramp: the area under the a-versus-t curve is the velocity gained.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A motorcyclist at a Chennai traffic light. From green light to cruising, she uses increasing throttle — acceleration climbs, then settles, then drops to zero once she reaches cruising speed. Her velocity is the running integral of her ever-changing acceleration.

formulas.basic_definition: a(t) = dv/dt   =>   v(t) = u + integral from 0 to t of a(t') dt'
formulas.position_from_velocity: x(t) = x0 + integral from 0 to t of v(t') dt'
formulas.example_a_2t: If a(t) = 2t, v(t) = u + t^2, x(t) = x0 + u t + t^3/3

constraints: Kinematic equations (v = u + at, s = ut + 0.5at^2) assume CONSTANT a — they do not apply here
Velocity at any time t = u + integral of a(t) dt, evaluated from 0 to t
Position at any time t = x0 + integral of v(t) dt, evaluated from 0 to t
Two integrations are needed: a -> v -> x

STATE_1.title: The Constant-a Assumption Breaks — Now What?

scene_composition.text:
Scenario

Driver on NH-48 varies the accelerator.
At t = 0, a = 0 (no press).
At t = 1 s, a = 2 m/s^2.
At t = 2 s, a = 4 m/s^2.
At t = 5 s, a = 10 m/s^2.

General pattern
   a(t) = 2 t  m/s^2

Acceleration is NOT constant.
It INCREASES with time.

You cannot use v = u + a t,
because 'a' is not a single number.

Solution recipe

(1) Given a(t), integrate ONCE
    to get v(t).

(2) Integrate v(t) AGAIN
    to get x(t).

(3) Integration constants come
    from initial conditions
    (v(0), x(0) known).

The constant-a case is a SPECIAL
case where a(t) = a_0 is a flat line.
Everything we do here generalises it.

tts_sentences.text_en: Imagine a driver on NH-48 playing with the accelerator. At t equals zero, his foot is off the pedal, so a is zero. A second later, a is two. Another second later, a is four. The acceleration itself is growing with time.
In symbols, a of t equals two t. Acceleration is a function of time, not a constant number.
This breaks our kinematic equations. v equals u plus a t assumes a is the same throughout. Here it is not.

Calculus handles this exactly. Given a as a function of t, integrate ONCE to get velocity as a function of t. Integrate the velocity AGAIN to get position as a function of t. Two integrations, each with an integration constant that you determine from initial conditions.
The constant-a case is just the special case where a of t equals a constant number. The calculus approach we are about to develop generalises the entire kinematics toolbox.

STATE_2.title: First Integration — From a(t) to v(t)

scene_composition.text:
Step 1: integrate a(t) to get v(t)

Start from a(t) = dv/dt.

Multiply both sides by dt
  dv = a(t) dt

Integrate both sides from t = 0 to t
  integral dv  =  integral of a(t) dt
  v(t) - v(0)  =  integral from 0 to t of a(t') dt'

With u = v(0) as initial velocity
  v(t)  =  u + integral from 0 to t of a(t') dt'

For our example a(t') = 2 t'
  integral 2 t' dt'  from 0 to t
    =  t^2

So  v(t)  =  u + t^2
With u = 0:
  v(t) = t^2

At t = 3 s, v = 9 m/s.
At t = 5 s, v = 25 m/s.

Graphical reading

On an a(t) graph, the AREA under
the curve from 0 to t equals the
velocity GAINED.

Area under a = 2t from 0 to t
  is a triangle of base t and
  height 2t
  area = (1/2)(t)(2t) = t^2

Same answer as the integration.
First Integration — From a(t) to v(t)

tts_sentences.text_en: Step one — integrate a of t to get v of t.
Start from the definition. Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. In equation form, d v over d t equals a of t. Rearrange — d v equals a of t d t.
Integrate both sides from t equals zero to some later time t. The left side gives v of t minus v at zero. The right side is the integral of a of t prime d t prime from zero to t.
Calling the initial velocity u, we get v of t equals u plus the integral of a of t prime d t prime from zero to t. That is the working formula.

For our example, a of t prime is two t prime. Integral from zero to t of two t prime d t prime is t squared. So v of t is u plus t squared. With u equals zero, v of t equals t squared. At three seconds, v is nine metres per second. At five seconds, twenty-five metres per second.

A useful shortcut — on an a-versus-t graph, the area under the curve from zero to t is the velocity gained. The triangle under a equals two t has base t and height two t, so area is t squared. Same answer.

STATE_3.title: Second Integration — From v(t) to x(t)

scene_composition.text:
Step 2: integrate v(t) to get x(t)

Start from  v = dx/dt
  dx = v(t) dt

Integrate from 0 to t
  x(t) - x(0)  =  integral from 0 to t of v(t') dt'

With x0 = x(0) as initial position
  x(t)  =  x0 + integral from 0 to t of v(t') dt'

For our example v(t') = t'^2
  integral t'^2 dt'  from 0 to t
    =  t^3 / 3

So  x(t)  =  x0 + t^3 / 3
With x0 = 0
  x(t) = t^3 / 3

At t = 3 s, x = 9 m.
At t = 5 s, x = 41.67 m.
Second Integration — From v(t) to x(t)
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Step two — integrate v of t to get x of t. Same idea, one level deeper.
Velocity is the rate of change of position, d x over d t equals v of t. So d x equals v of t d t.
Integrate from zero to t. x of t minus x at zero equals integral of v of t prime d t prime.
With the initial position x-zero, x of t equals x-zero plus the integral of v of t prime from zero to t.
For our example, v of t prime is t prime squared. Integral is t cubed over three. So x of t equals x-zero plus t cubed over three.

With x-zero equal to zero, x of t is t cubed over three. At three seconds, x is nine metres. At five seconds, about forty-two metres. Position grows much faster than it does under constant acceleration, because the acceleration itself was growing.

STATE_4.title: The Workflow — Two Integrals, Then Read Off the Answer

scene_composition.text:
Workflow for a(t) problems

(1) Identify a(t) from the problem.
    Read the function of t.

(2) Integrate a(t) once.
    v(t) = u + integral of a(t') dt' from 0 to t.

(3) Integrate v(t) again.
    x(t) = x0 + integral of v(t') dt' from 0 to t.

(4) Plug in initial conditions (u, x0)
    and answer the question.

No assumption of constant a.
Works for ANY integrable a(t).

The 3-equation kinematics you memorised
emerges naturally when a(t) = a (constant).
The Workflow — Two Integrals, Then Read Off the Answer
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the workflow for any a-as-a-function-of-t problem.
Step one — read a of t from the problem. Could be a straight line, a polynomial, a sinusoid — whatever the problem hands you.
Step two — integrate once to get v of t. Step three — integrate again to get x of t. Step four — use initial conditions to fix u and x-zero, then answer the question.

This workflow handles any integrable a of t. It is strictly more powerful than the constant-a kinematic equations — those are just the special case when a is constant, and the integrations give t and half t squared factors.
If a problem gives you an a as an explicit function of t, do not try the constant-a equations. Integrate, get v, integrate again, get x. Every JEE question on this topic is built on this pattern.

epic_c_branches.misconception: v = u + a t still works when a is a function of time
trigger_phrases: use v = u + at with variable a
can I plug a(t) into constant-a formula
why does kinematic equation give wrong answer
a is not constant but I'll use the old formula

STATE_1.title: Your Belief — Use the Kinematic Equations Anyway

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

I know v = u + a t.
For a(t) = 2t, at t = 5 s,
a is 10 m/s^2.

So v = 0 + 10 * 5 = 50 m/s?

Actually the correct answer
(from integration) is v = 25 m/s.

Huge gap. The formula does NOT
work when a changes over time.
Your Belief — Use the Kinematic Equations Anyway
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: A very common error — reach for the familiar v equals u plus a t even when a is changing.
At t equals five seconds, a of t equals two t gives a equals ten. Plug in. v equals zero plus ten times five equals fifty metres per second. Done?
The correct answer from proper integration is twenty-five, not fifty. The kinematic equation overshoots by a factor of two. Let us see why.

STATE_2.title: Why the Equation Fails — It Multiplies by t, Not Integrates

scene_composition.text:
v = u + a t comes from
  v - u = integral of a from 0 to t
       = a * t  (ONLY when a is constant)

When a varies over time, the integral is
NOT simply a * t. The value a changes
during the interval.

For a(t) = 2t, between 0 and 5 s
  integral of 2t dt = t^2 from 0 to 5
  = 25
  NOT 10 * 5 = 50

Using the constant-a formula with
the FINAL value of a counts the
full peak acceleration for the
entire interval — overestimating.

Integration correctly averages
the changing a over the time window.
Why the Equation Fails — It Multiplies by t, Not Integrates
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Remember where v equals u plus a t came from. It was derived assuming a is a constant throughout the interval. The integral of a constant a from zero to t is simply a times t.
When a varies, you cannot pull it out of the integral. The integral of two t from zero to five is t squared evaluated from zero to five, which is twenty-five. Not ten times five.

Using the constant-a formula with the final a value treats the entire window at peak acceleration. That is an overestimate — the car was NOT at acceleration ten for the full five seconds; it started at zero and ramped up.
Integration handles this correctly because it sums up all the small instantaneous contributions, each with the actual a at that instant. That is the calculus answer and the right one.

STATE_3.title: The Rule — Always Integrate When a Is Non-Constant

scene_composition.text:
Rule

If a is a CONSTANT in the interval
  -> use v = u + a t.

If a is a FUNCTION of t (varies)
  -> use v(t) = u + integral of a(t) dt from 0 to t.

If a is a FUNCTION of x or v (coming up)
  -> use different tricks (see
     a_function_of_x and a_function_of_v
     concepts).

First read the problem, then pick the tool.
The Rule — Always Integrate When a Is Non-Constant
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: The simple rule — if a is constant in the relevant interval, use v equals u plus a t. If a varies with time, always integrate.

Before you pick any formula, first read the problem. If the problem gives you a as a function of t — a equals two t, a equals three sine t, a equals five minus t — that is your signal to reach for integration.
The constant-a kinematic equations are a special case. They work when they work. Integration works always.

regeneration_variants.label: Sinusoidal acceleration
regeneration_variants.physical_world: a(t) = 5 cos(t). v(t) = u + 5 sin(t). x(t) = x0 + u t - 5 cos(t) + 5. Oscillating motion.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Two successive integrations with correct initial conditions

regeneration_variants.label: Constant a is a special case
regeneration_variants.physical_world: a(t) = a_0 (constant). Integrate: v(t) = u + a_0 t. Integrate again: x(t) = x0 + u t + 0.5 a_0 t^2. Recovers the standard kinematic equations.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Calculus framework generalises the constant-a kinematic equations
